text: The surgical team prepares for an extracorporeal Pringle maneuver by placing a tape around the hepatic hilum, which will be tied using a chest tube passed through a 12mm trocar.
The surgeon begins the procedure with hilar dissection, isolating the right hepatic artery and suspending it on a tape for clear visualization.
The surgeon isolates and suspends the right portal vein, temporarily clamping both the right portal vein and artery with a bulldog clamp to identify the cantilever line.
The surgeon performs intraoperative ultrasound to identify the liver lesion and marks the transection line with adequate margins.
The surgeon releases the bulldog clamps and divides both the right hepatic artery and portal vein using Hem-o-lok clips.
The surgeon isolates accessory hepatic veins and controls them using clips or a stapler, depending on the size of the veins.
The surgeon immobilizes the right lobe of the liver, isolates the right hepatic vein, encircles it, and divides it using a vascular stapler.
The surgeon divides the liver parenchyma using the classic Kelly crash technique, employing a bipolar Maryland dissector and harmonic scalpel to separate the tissue.
The surgeon isolates the right biliary duct within the liver parenchyma and divides it using hemlock clips for safety.
The surgeon divides another branch of the right hepatic vein, completing the right hepatectomy.
The surgeon moves to the right colon and ligates the ileocolic vessels, securing them with surgical ties.
The surgeon staples the last silla loop and the transverse colon, then collects the specimen in a land bag.
The surgical team prepares for a manual intracorporeal anastomosis using a running 3-0 V-Loc suture.
The final specimen is displayed, along with a view of the abdomen showing the completed surgical field at the end of the procedure.
